| |`a[i]`<br>L-Value |`a[i]`<br>R-Value |`a[i..]`<br>&nbsp; |`a[i..j]`<br>L-Value |`a[i..j]`<br>R-Value |`REMOVE()`<br>&nbsp; |`COPY`<br>&nbsp; |`FOR`<br>&nbsp; |
|:--|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
|Array ADTs|(1)|(2)|(3)|(1)|(2)|(4)|(5)|(5)|

**Required Bindings:**

(1) `ATSTORE` &nbsp;&nbsp; (2) `ATVALUE` &nbsp;&nbsp; (3) `ATINSERT` &nbsp;&nbsp; (4) `ATREMOVE` &nbsp;&nbsp; (5) `LENGTH`/`COUNT`, `ATSTORE`, `ATVALUE` 

| |`dict[key]`<br>L-Value |`dict[key]`<br>R-Value |`REMOVE()`<br>&nbsp; |`key` `IN` `dict`<br>&nbsp; |`COPY`<br>&nbsp; |`FOR`<br>&nbsp; |
|:--|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
|Dictionary ADTs|(1)|(2)|(3)|(2)|(4)|(4)|

**Required Bindings:**

(1) `STORE` &nbsp;&nbsp; (2) `VALUE` &nbsp;&nbsp; (3) `ATREMOVE` &nbsp;&nbsp; (4) `FIRST`, `LAST`, `PREV`, `NEXT`, `STORE`, `VALUE` 


### ADT Syntax Bindings ###

| |`NEW`<br>`RETAIN`<br>`RELEASE` |`READ`<br>`WRITE`<br>&nbsp; |`APPEND`<br>&nbsp;<br>&nbsp; |`REMOVE`<br>&nbsp;<br>&nbsp; |`COUNT`<br>&nbsp;<br>&nbsp; |`LENGTH`<br>&nbsp;<br>&nbsp; |
|:--|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
|Any ADT|&cir;|&cir;|&mdash;|&mdash;|&mdash;|&mdash;|
|Array ADTs|&cir;|&cir;|&cir;|&mdash;|&cir;|&cir;|
|Dictionary ADTs|&cir;|&cir;|&mdash;|&cir;|&cir;|&mdash;|

| |`FIRST()`<br>`LAST()`|`PREV()`<br>`NEXT()` |`ATVALUE`<br>`ATSTORE` |`ATINSERT`<br>`ATREMOVE` |`VALUE`<br>`STORE` |`TLIMIT()`<br>&nbsp; |
|:--|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
|Array ADTs|&mdash;|&mdash;|&cir;|&cir;|&mdash;|&cir;|
|Dictionary ADTs|&cir;|&cir;|&mdash;|&mdash;|&cir;|&cir;|

---

# Chapter 8 : Type Compatibility #
This is the chapter headline

*Type compatibility* is a relation between two types *T*<sub>*1*</sub> and *T*<sub>*2*</sub> that determines whether an entity *e*<sub>*1*</sub> of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*1*</sub> may be assigned to, copied to, passed to or used together in an expression with an entity *e*<sub>*2*</sub> of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*2*</sub>.

Type compatibility thus determines the compatibility of two entities by their associated types. The compatibility relation between any two entities is congruent to the compatibility relation of their types. Any compatibility relation is always transitive, but not necessarily commutative. There are five compatibility relations.

#### Assignment Compatibility ####

*Assignment compatibility* is a relation between two types *T*<sub>*1*</sub> and *T*<sub>*2*</sub> that determines whether an entity *e*<sub>*1*</sub> of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*1*</sub> may be assigned to an entity *e*<sub>*2*</sub> of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*2*</sub>. The relation is denoted by the `=:`&nbsp;symbol, pronounced *"assignment compatible to"*.

* *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *e*<sub>*1*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &and; *e*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &#8658; *e*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *e*<sub>*2*</sub>


#### Copy Compatibility ####

*Copy compatibility* is a relation between two types *T*<sub>*1*</sub> and *T*<sub>*2*</sub> that determines whether an entity *e*<sub>*1*</sub> of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*1*</sub> may be copied to an entity *e*<sub>*2*</sub> of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*2*</sub>. The relation is denoted by the `>:`&nbsp;symbol, pronounced *"copy compatible to"*.

* *T*<sub>*1*</sub> >: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *e*<sub>*1*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &and; *e*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &#8658; *e*<sub>*1*</sub> >: *e*<sub>*2*</sub>


#### By-Value Passing Compatibility ####

*By-value passing compatibility* is a relation between two types *T*<sub>*1*</sub> and *T*<sub>*2*</sub> that determines whether an entity *e*<sub>*1*</sub> of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*1*</sub> may be passed to a formal by-value parameter *e*<sub>*2*</sub> of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*2*</sub>. The relation is denoted by the `≈:`&nbsp;symbol, pronounced *"by-value passing compatible to"*.

* *T*<sub>*1*</sub> ≈: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *e*<sub>*1*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &and; *e*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &#8658; *e*<sub>*1*</sub> ≈: *e*<sub>*2*</sub>


#### By-Reference Passing Compatibility ####

*By-reference passing compatibility* is a relation between two types *T*<sub>*1*</sub> and *T*<sub>*2*</sub> that determines whether an entity *e*<sub>*1*</sub> of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*1*</sub> may be passed to a formal by-reference parameter *e*<sub>*2*</sub> of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*2*</sub>. The relation is denoted by the `^:`&nbsp;symbol, pronounced *"by-reference passing compatible to"*.

* *T*<sub>*1*</sub> ^: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *e*<sub>*1*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &and; *e*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &#8658; *e*<sub>*1*</sub> ^: *e*<sub>*2*</sub>


#### Expression Compatibility ####

*Expression compatibility* is a relation between two types *T*<sub>*1*</sub> and *T*<sub>*2*</sub> that determines whether two entities *e*<sub>*1*</sub> of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*1*</sub> and *e*<sub>*2*</sub> of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*2*</sub> may be used together in a binary expression. The relation is commutative. It is denoted by the `○`&nbsp;symbol, pronounced *"expression compatible with"*.

* *T*<sub>*1*</sub> ○ *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *e*<sub>*1*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &and; *e*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &#8658; *e*<sub>*1*</sub> ○ *e*<sub>*2*</sub>


#### Hierarchy of Compatibility Relations ####

Assignment compatibility implies all other compatibility relations. Copy compatibility implies by-value passing compatibility. Passing compatibility implies expression compatibility.

* *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &#8658; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> >: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &#8658; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> ≈: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &#8658; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> ○ *T*<sub>*2*</sub>
* *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &#8658; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> ^: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &#8658; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> ○ *T*<sub>*2*</sub>


### Type Regimes ###

A type regime is a set of rules that govern type compatibility.

The following type regimes are in use

#### Strict Name Equivalence ####

Under strict name equivalence, types are equivalent if they are identical types.

* *T*<sub>*1*</sub> = *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &#8658; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>


#### Super-Type Equivalence ####

Under super-type equivalence, a type is upwards compatible with its super-type, but a super-type is not downwards compatible with its subtype.

* *T*<sub>*1*</sub> **is-subtype-of** *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &#8658; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; &not; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>


#### Value Type Equivalence ####

Under value type equivalence, collection types are copy compatible if their value types are compatible.

* *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*collection*</sub> &and; **value-type** *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: **value-type** *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &#8658; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> >: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> >: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>


#### Target Type Equivalence ####

Under target type equivalence, pointer types are compatible if their target types are compatible.

* *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*pointer*</sub> &and; **target-type** *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: **target-type** *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &#8658; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub>


#### Structural Equivalence ####

Under structural equivalence, types are compatible if they have identical structure.

* *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &#8792; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> => *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>


#### Limited Equivalence ####

A type regime is limited if it only applies to a subset of compatibility relations.



## Compatibility by Type Classification ##

Except for type `OCTETSEQ`, all types follow strict name equivalence by default.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*any*</sub> &#8726; *T*<sub>*octetseq*</sub> | *T*<sub>*1*</sub> = *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>

The type regime may further be relaxed depending on the classification of the type.


### Alias Type Compatibility ###

An alias type is always identical to the type of which it is an alias.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*any*</sub> | *T*<sub>*1*</sub> **is-alias-of** *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &equiv; *T*<sub>*2*</sub>


### Derived Type Compatibility ###

A derived type is by definition incompatible with the type from which it is derived.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*any*</sub> | *T*<sub>*1*</sub> **is-derivative-of** *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : &not; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; &not; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>


### Anonymous Type Compatibility ###

Anonymous types with the same canonical type declaration are identical.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*anon*</sub> | *T*<sub>*1*</sub> **is-canonically-equal-to** *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &equiv; *T*<sub>*2*</sub>


### Enumeration Type Compatibility ###

Enumeration types follow strict name equivalence.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*enum*</sub> | *T*<sub>*1*</sub> = *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>

Enumeration types further follow super-type equivalence.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*enum*</sub> | *T*<sub>*2*</sub> **is-extension-of** *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &or; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> **is-subtype-of** *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; &not; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>


### Set Type Compatibility ###

Set types follow strict name equivalence.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*set*</sub> | *T*<sub>*1*</sub> = *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>

Set types further follow value type equivalence.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*set*</sub> | **value-type** *T*<sub>*1*</sub> = **value-type** *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> >: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> >: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>
* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*set*</sub> | **value-type** *T*<sub>*1*</sub> ⊂ **value-type** *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> >: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; &not; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> >: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>


### Array Type Compatibility ###

Array types follow strict name equivalence.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*array*</sub> | *T*<sub>*1*</sub> = *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>

Array types further follow value-type equivalence.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*array*</sub> | **value-type** *T*<sub>*1*</sub> = **value-type** *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> >: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> >: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>
* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*array*</sub> | **value-type** *T*<sub>*1*</sub> ⊂ **value-type** *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> >: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; &not; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> >: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>


### Record Type Compatibility ###

Record types follow strict name equivalence.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*record*</sub> | *T*<sub>*1*</sub> = *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>

Extensible record types further follow limited super-type equivalence.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*extrec*</sub> | *T*<sub>*2*</sub> **is-extension-of** *T*<sub>*1*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*2*</sub> ^: *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &and; &not; *T*<sub>*1*</sub> ^: *T*<sub>*2*</sub>


### Pointer Type Compatibility ###

Opaque pointer types follow strict name equivalence.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*opaque*</sub> | *T*<sub>*1*</sub> = *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>

Other pointer types follow target type name equivalence.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*pointer*</sub> &#8726; *T*<sub>*opaque*</sub> | **target-type** *T*<sub>*1*</sub> = **target-type** *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>

Extensible record pointer types follow super-type equivalence.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*recptr*</sub> | *T*<sub>*1*</sub> **is-extension-of** *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; &not; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>

Given pointer types *T*<sub>*1*</sub> and *T*<sub>*2*</sub>, *T*<sub>*1*</sub> is a type extension of *T*<sub>*2*</sub> if the target type of *T*<sub>*1*</sub> is a type extension of the target type of *T*<sub>*2*</sub>.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*recptr*</sub> : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> **is-extension-of** *T*<sub>*2*</sub> if *T*<sub>*1*</sub>&prime; **is-extension-of** *T*<sub>*2*</sub>&prime; <br> where *T*<sub>*1*</sub>&prime; = **target-type** *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub>&prime; = **target-type** *T*<sub>*2*</sub>


### Procedure Type Compatibility ###

Procedure types follow structural equivalence.

* any two procedure types are compatible if their signatures match
* the type of a procedure is the anonymous procedure type that matches its signature


### Numeric Type Compatibility ###

Numeric types follow strict name equivalence.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*scalar*</sub> | *T*<sub>*1*</sub> = *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>

Numeric subrange types follow super-type equivalence.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*scalar*</sub> | *T*<sub>*1*</sub> **is-subtype-of** *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; &not; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>


### Machine Type Compatibility ###

Machine types follow strict name equivalence only.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*machine*</sub> | *T*<sub>*1*</sub> = *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>


### Address Type Compatibility ###

Address types follow strict name equivalence only.

* &forall; *T*<sub>*1*</sub>, *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &in; { *T*<sub>*address*</sub> | *T*<sub>*1*</sub> = *T*<sub>*2*</sub> } : *T*<sub>*1*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*2*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> =: *T*<sub>*1*</sub>


### Literal Compatibility ###

The compatibility of a literal value cannot be determined by type because literals are not associated with any specific type. Instead, literal compatibility is defined as follows:

A literal value *L* is compatible with type *T* if *L* does not overflow *T* and if
* *L* is a character code literal and *T* is a character type, or
* *L* is a whole number literal and *T* is a machine type, cardinal type or integer type, or
* *L* is a real number literal and *T* is a real number type, or
* *L* is a non-empty structured literal and structurally equivalent to *T*

An empty structured literal *L* is compatible with type *T* if
* *L* is the empty string `""` and *T* &in; *T*<sub>*string*</sub>, or
* *L* is the empty collection `{}` and *T* &in; *T*<sub>*set*</sub> &cup; *T*<sub>*array*</sub> &#8726; *T*<sub>*string*</sub>

*Type compatibility* is a relation between two types *T*<sub>*1*</sub> and *T*<sub>*2*</sub> that determines whether an entity *e*<sub>*1*</sub> of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*1*</sub> may be assigned to, copied to, passed to or used together in an expression with an entity *e*<sub>*2*</sub> of type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*2*</sub>.

Type compatibility thus determines the compatibility of two entities by their associated types. The compatibility relation between any two entities is congruent to the compatibility relation of their types. Any compatibility relation is always transitive, but not necessarily commutative. There are five compatibility relations.

---

# Chapter 9 : Predefined Identifiers #
This is the chapter headline

Predefined identifiers are language defined and built-in. They are pervasive, that is, they are visible in every scope without import. Pervasive identifiers do not have qualified names and are always referenced unqualified. Unlike earlier versions of Modula-2, pervasive identifiers are now reserved and may not be redefined.

The following identifiers are predefined:

[`NIL`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#nil),
[`TRUE`](https://https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#false-and-true),
[`FALSE`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#false-and-true),
[`BOOLEAN`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#boolean),
[`OCTET`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#octet),
[`CHAR`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#char),
[`UNICHAR`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#unichar),
[`CARDINAL`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#cardinal),
[`LONGCARD`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#longcard),
[`INTEGER`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#integer),
[`LONGINT`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#longint),
[`REAL`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#real),
[`LONGREAL`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#longreal),
[`APPEND`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#append),
[`INSERT`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#insert),
[`REMOVE`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#remove),
[`CHR`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#chr),
[`UCHR`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#uchr),
[`COLLATION`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#collation),
[`ORD`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#ord),
[`ODD`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#odd),
[`ABS`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#abs),
[`SGN`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#sgn),
[`MIN`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#min),
[`MAX`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#max),
[`LOG2`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#log2),
[`POW2`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#pow2),
[`ENTIER`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#entier),
[`PRED`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#pred),
[`SUCC`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#succ),
[`PTR`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#ptr),
[`CAPACITY`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#capacity),
[`COUNT`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#count),
[`LENGTH`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#length),
[`FIRST`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#first),
[`LAST`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#last),
[`PREV`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#prev),
[`NEXT`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#next),
[`TMIN`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#tmin),
[`TMAX`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#tmax),
[`TSIZE`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#tsize),
[`TLIMIT`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#tlimit);


## Predefined Constants ##

### NIL ###

Identifier `NIL` represents an invalid pointer or address value. It is per definition compatible with any pointer type, including all opaque pointer types and all procedure types.


### TRUE and FALSE ###

Identifiers `TRUE` and `FALSE` represent the values of type `BOOLEAN`.


## Predefined Types ##

### BOOLEAN ###

Type `BOOLEAN` is an [[enumerated type|Language-Description#type-enumerated]]. It represents logical truth values used in boolean expressions. It is defined as

```Modula-2
TYPE BOOLEAN = ( FALSE, TRUE );
```
The order is significant. `ORD(FALSE)` is always zero, `ORD(TRUE)` is always one.


### OCTET ###

Type `OCTET` is a [[cardinal type|Language-Description#type-cardinal]].

It represents the smallest addressable unit which is per definition 8 bits.

Its value range is always `[0..255]`.

Whole number literals are compatible with type `OCTET`.


### CHAR ###

Type `CHAR` is a [[countable type|Language-Description#type-countable]].

It represents the 7-bit character codes of [ISO 646](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO/IEC_646) (aka ASCII).

Its value range is always `[0u0..0u7F]`.

Quoted literals of exactly one ISO 646 code point are compatible with type `CHAR`.


### UNICHAR ###

Type `UNICHAR` represents the 32-bit character codes of [ISO 10646](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universal_Coded_Character_Set) UCS-4.

Its value range is `[0u0..0u10FFFF]`.

Quoted literals of exactly one ISO 10646 code point are compatible with type `UNICHAR`.


### CARDINAL ###

Type `CARDINAL` is a [[cardinal type|Language-Description#type-cardinal]]. It represents unsigned whole numbers.

Its value range is `[0..TMAX(CARDINAL)]` where `TMAX(CARDINAL) = POW2(8*TSIZE(CARDINAL)) - 1`

The value of `TSIZE(CARDINAL)` is implementation defined, where `TSIZE(CARDINAL) <= TSIZE(LONGCARD)`.

Whole number literals are compatible with type `CARDINAL`.


### LONGCARD ###

Type `LONGCARD` is a [[cardinal type|Language-Description#type-cardinal]]. It represents unsigned whole numbers with extended range.

Its value range is `[0..TMAX(LONGCARD)]` where `TMAX(LONGCARD) = POW2(8*TSIZE(LONGCARD)) - 1`

The value of `TSIZE(LONGCARD)` is implementation defined where `TSIZE(LONGCARD) >= TSIZE(ADDRESS)`

Whole number literals are compatible with type `LONGCARD`.


### INTEGER ###

Type `INTEGER` is an [[integer type|Language-Description#type-integer]]. It represents signed whole numbers.

Its value range is `[TMIN(INTEGER)..TMAX(INTEGER)]`.

The internal representation of type `INTEGER` is always in two's complement. Thus

* `TMIN(INTEGER) = (-1) * POW2(8*TSIZE(INTEGER) - 1)`
* `TMAX(INTEGER) = POW2(8*TSIZE(INTEGER) - 1) - 1`

The value of `TSIZE(INTEGER)` is always equal to `TSIZE(CARDINAL)`.

Whole number literals are compatible with type `INTEGER`.


### LONGINT ###

Type `LONGINT` is an [[integer type|Language-Description#type-integer]]. It represents signed whole numbers with extended range.

Its value range is `[TMIN(LONGINT)..TMAX(LONGINT)]`.

The internal representation of type `LONGINT` is always in two's complement. Thus

* `TMIN(LONGINT) = (-1) * POW2(8*TSIZE(LONGINT) - 1)`
* `TMAX(LONGINT) = POW2(8*TSIZE(LONGINT) - 1) - 1`

The value of `TSIZE(LONGINT)` is always equal to `TSIZE(LONGCARD)`.

Whole number literals are compatible with type `LONGINT`.


### REAL ###

Type `REAL` is a [[real number type|Language-Description#type-real]]. It represents real numbers with standard range and precision.

Its value range is `[TMIN(REAL)..TMAX(REAL)]`.

The internal representation of type `REAL` is implementation defined where

* `TMIN(REAL) <= TMIN(LONGINT)` and `TMAX(REAL) >= TMAX(LONGCARD)`

IEEE 754 [single](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Single-precision_floating-point_format)
or [double](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Double-precision_floating-point_format)
precision format is recommended for type `REAL`.

Real number literals are compatible with type `REAL`.


### LONGREAL ###

Type `LONGREAL` is a [[real number type|Language-Description#type-real]]. It represents real numbers with extended range or precision or both.

Its value range is `[TMIN(LONGREAL)..TMAX(LONGREAL)]`.

The internal representation of type `LONGREAL` is implementation defined where

* `TMIN(LONGREAL) <= TMIN(REAL)` and `TMAX(LONGREAL) >= TMAX(REAL)`
* the precision of type `REAL` must not exceed that of type `LONGREAL`

IEEE 754 [double](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Double-precision_floating-point_format)
or [quadruple](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quadruple-precision_floating-point_format)
precision format is recommended for type `LONGREAL`.

Real number literals are compatible with type `LONGREAL`.


## Predefined Procedures ##

### APPEND ###

Procedure `APPEND` appends one or more values to an array.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE APPEND ( VAR array : ArrayType; values : ARGLIST OF ValueType );
```
Values to be appended must be of the component type of the array.


### INSERT ###

Procedure `INSERT` inserts one or more values of a given list into a set.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE INSERT ( VAR set : SetType; values : ARGLIST OF ValueType );
```
Values passed in the list must be of the value type of the set.


### REMOVE ###

Procedure `REMOVE` has multiple signatures, depending on the type of its first argument.

If its first argument is a set, it removes the values in a given list from the set.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE REMOVE ( VAR set : SetType; values : ARGLIST OF ValueType );
```
Values passed in the list must be of the value type of the set.

If its first argument is a dictionary, it removes the keys in a given list along with their values from the dictionary.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE REMOVE ( VAR dict : DictType; values : ARGLIST OF KeyType );
```
Values passed in the list must be of the key type of the dictionary.

If its first argument is an array, it removes the values addressed by a given index range from the array.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE REMOVE ( VAR array : ArrayType; startIndex, endIndex : LONGCARD );
```
Any relative (negative) indices passed are translated into absolute (positive) indices by the language processor.


## Predefined Functions ##

### CHR ###

Function `CHR` returns the character whose character code is its argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE CHR ( n : T ) : CHAR;
```
where 0 &le; *n* &le; 127 &and; *T* &in; *T*<sub>*cardinal*</sub>. 


### UCHR ###

Function `UCHR` returns the unicode character whose character code is its argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE UCHR ( n : T ) : UNICHAR;
```
where 0 &le; *n* &le; 0x10FFFF &and; *T* &in; *T*<sub>*cardinal*</sub>. 


### COLLATION ###

Function `COLLATION` returns the index within the prevailing collation sequence for a given character.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE COLLATION ( ch : T ) : LONGCARD;
```
where *T* &in; *T*<sub>*char*</sub>. 

The return values are determined as follows:
* for tabulator `0u09` and newline `0u0A`, the index for whitespace `0u20` is looked up and returned
* for all other control characters, `TMAX(LONGCARD)` is returned &mdash; they are ignored by the collation algorithm
* for characters in range `[0u20..0u7E]`, the return value is looked up in the prevailing collation table
* for accented letters, the return value equals that of the corresponding unaccented letter
* for all other characters, the ordinal value of the character is returned

The default collation sequence for printable 7-bit characters is:
```
␣0123456789@aAbBcCdDeEfFgGhHiIjJkKlLmMnNoOpPqQrRsStTuUvVwWxXyYzZ!"#$%&’()*+,-./:;<=>?[\]∧_‘{|}~
```

The prevailing collation order may be changed by import of a collation library.


### ORD ###

Function `ORD` returns the ordinal value of its countable argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE ORD ( value : T ) : LONGCARD;
```
where *T* &in; *T*<sub>*countable*</sub>.


### ODD ###

Function `ODD` returns `TRUE` if its argument is [[odd|Language-Description#odd-value]], otherwise `FALSE`.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE ODD ( value : T ) : BOOLEAN;
```
where *T* &in; *T*<sub>*whole*</sub> &cup; *T*<sub>*machine*</sub>.


### ABS ###

Function `ABS` returns the absolute value of its scalar argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE ABS ( x : T1 ) : T2;
```
where *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*signed*</sub> &cap; *T*<sub>*scalar*</sub>
&and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> = *T*<sub>*1*</sub>.


### SGN ###

Function `SGN` returns the [[signum value|Language-Description#signum-value]]
of its argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE SGN ( value : T1 ) : T2;
```
where *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*signed*</sub> &cap; *T*<sub>*scalar*</sub>
&and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> = *T*<sub>*1*</sub>.


### MIN ###

Function `MIN` returns the smallest value in its variadic argument list.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE MIN ( values : ARGLIST OF T1 ) : T2;
```
where *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*countable*</sub> &cup; *T*<sub>*scalar*</sub>
&and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> = *T*<sub>*1*</sub>.


### MAX ###

Function `MAX` returns the largest value in its variadic argument list.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE MAX ( values : ARGLIST OF T1 ) : T2;
```
where *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*countable*</sub> &cup; *T*<sub>*scalar*</sub>
&and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> = *T*<sub>*1*</sub>.


### LOG2 ###

Function `LOG2` returns the truncated [binary logarithm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binary_logarithm)
of its cardinal argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE LOG2 ( n : T1 ) : T2;
```
where *n* &ne; 0 &and;
*T*<sub>*1*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*cardinal*</sub>
&and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> = *T*<sub>*1*</sub>.


### POW2 ###

Function `POW2` returns the value of its argument raised to the [power of 2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Power_of_two).

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE POW2 ( n : T1 ) : T2;
```
where *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*cardinal*</sub>
&and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> = *T*<sub>*1*</sub>.


### ENTIER ###

Function `ENTIER` returns the [[largest integer|Language-Description#largest-integer-or-entier]] or **entier** of its real number argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE ENTIER ( r : T1 ) : T2;
```
where *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*real*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> = *T*<sub>*1*</sub>.


### PRED ###

Function `PRED` returns the predecessor of its countable argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE PRED ( value : T1 ) : T2;
```
where *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*countable*</sub>
&and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> = *T*<sub>*1*</sub>.


### SUCC ###

Function `SUCC` returns the successor of its countable argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE SUCC ( value : T1 ) : T2;
```
where *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*countable*</sub>
&and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> = *T*<sub>*1*</sub>.


### PTR ###

Function `PTR` returns a pointer to its argument.
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE PTR ( value : T1 ) : POINTER TO T2;
```
where *T*<sub>*1*</sub> &in; *T*<sub>*any*</sub>
&and; *T*<sub>*2*</sub> = *T*<sub>*1*</sub>


### CAPACITY ###

Function `CAPACITY` returns the [[capacity|Language-Description#capacity]] of its argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE CAPACITY ( entity : T ) : LONGCARD;
```
where *T* &in; *T*<sub>*collection*</sub>


### COUNT ###

Function `COUNT` returns the [[cardinality|Language-Description#cardinality]] of its argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE COUNT ( entity : T ) : LONGCARD;
```
where *T* &in; *T*<sub>*collection*</sub> &#8726; *T*<sub>*string*</sub> &#8726; *T*<sub>*octetseq*</sub>

The argument must be either (1) an instance of a collection type that is not a string type, or (2) the identifier of a formal open array parameter whose value type is not a character type, or (3) the identifier of a variadic argument list.


### LENGTH ###

Function `LENGTH` returns the [[length|Language-Description#length]] of the character string or octet sequence denoted by its argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE LENGTH ( s : T ) : LONGCARD;
```
where *T* &in; *T*<sub>*collection*</sub> &#8726; *T*<sub>*octetseq*</sub>

The argument must be either (1) a value of a string type, or (2) the identifier of an pen array parameter whose value type is a character type, or (3) the identifier of a record field or formal parameter of type `OCTETSEQ`.


### FIRST ###

Function `FIRST` returns the first key of the dictionary denoted by its argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE FIRST ( dict : T1 ) : T2;
```
where *T* &in; *T*<sub>*dictionary*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>2</sub> **is-keytype-of** *T*<sub>1</sub>

The dictionary type must be a library ADT that implements key/value storage and provides a binding to `FIRST` otherwise a compile time error will occur. The function returns `NIL` if its argument is an empty dictionary.

### LAST ###

Function `LAST` returns the last key of the dictionary denoted by its argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE LAST ( dict : T1 ) : T2;
```
where *T* &in; *T*<sub>*dictionary*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>2</sub> **is-keytype-of** *T*<sub>1</sub>

The dictionary type must be a library ADT that implements key/value storage and provides a binding to `LAST` otherwise a compile time error will occur. The function returns `NIL` if its argument is an empty dictionary.


### PREV ###

Function `PREV` returns the key that precedes the given key of the given dictionary.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE PREV ( dict : T1; key : T2 ) : T2;
```
where *T* &in; *T*<sub>*dictionary*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>2</sub> **is-keytype-of** *T*<sub>1</sub>

The dictionary type must be a library ADT that implements key/value storage and provides a binding to `PREV` otherwise a compile time error will occur. The function returns `NIL` if the second argument is the first key.


### NEXT ###

Function `NEXT` returns the key that succeeds the given key of the given dictionary.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE NEXT ( dict : T1; key : T2 ) : T2;
```
where *T* &in; *T*<sub>*dictionary*</sub> &and; *T*<sub>2</sub> **is-keytype-of** *T*<sub>1</sub>

The dictionary type must be a library ADT that implements key/value storage and provides a binding to `NEXT` otherwise a compile time error will occur. The function returns `NIL` if the second argument is the last key.


## Built-in Macros ##

### TMIN ###

An invocation of macro `TMIN` is replaced by the smallest value of the type denoted by its argument.

```Modula-2
(*MACRO*) PROCEDURE TMIN ( TypeIdent ) : T;
```
where `TypeIdent` = **id** *T* &and; *T* &in; *T*<sub>*countable*</sub> &cup; *T*<sub>*scalar*</sub>


### TMAX ###

An invocation of macro `TMAX` is replaced by the largest value of the type denoted by its argument.

```Modula-2
(*MACRO*) PROCEDURE TMAX ( TypeIdent ) : T;
```
where `TypeIdent` = **id** *T* &and; *T* &in; *T*<sub>*countable*</sub> &cup; *T*<sub>*scalar*</sub>


### TSIZE ###

An invocation of macro `TSIZE` is replaced by the [[allocation size|Language-Description#allocation-size]] of the type denoted by its argument.

If the (primary) argument does **not** denote an indeterminate type, no second argument may be passed.
```Modula-2
(*MACRO*) PROCEDURE TSIZE ( TypeIdent ) : LONGCARD;
```

If the primary argument denotes a pointer type with indeterminate target, a second argument must be passed,
```Modula-2
(*MACRO*) PROCEDURE TSIZE ( TypeIdent; capacity : LONGCARD ) : LONGCARD;
```
and the result is the [[allocation size|Language-Description#allocation-size]] for the target with the capacity given by the second argument.

In any event, the (primary) argument must be a type identifier.


### TLIMIT ###

An invocation of macro `TLIMIT` is replaced by the [[capacity limit|Language-Description#capacity-limit]] of the type denoted by its argument.

```Modula-2
(*MACRO*) PROCEDURE TLIMIT ( TypeIdent ) : LONGCARD;
```
The argument must be the identifier of a collection type.


## Primitives ##

Primitives are **internal-use** procedures or macros that represent certain built-in syntax forms. They are **not** **callable** by user code but may be targeted by syntax bindings in ADT library modules. When a syntax form that is implemented by a primitive is found, it is internally replaced with an invocation of the associated primitive. The identifiers of primitives are reserved.

The following primitives are defined:

[`VALUE`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#Primitive-VALUE),
[`ATVALUE`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#Primitive-ATVALUE),
[`STORE`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#Primitive-STORE),
[`ATSTORE`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#Primitive-ATSTORE),
[`ATINSERT`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#Primitive-ATINSERT),
[`ATREMOVE`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#Primitive-ATREMOVE),
[`ALLOC`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#Primitive-ALLOC),
[`DEALLOC`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#Primitive-DEALLOC),
[`STDIN`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#Primitive-STDIN),
[`STDOUT`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Specification-(9)-:-Predefined-Identifiers#Primitive-STDOUT);


### Primitive VALUE ###

Primitive `VALUE` implements R-value subscript designators for dictionary types.
It returns the value stored for a given key in a key/value collection.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE VALUE ( CONST dict : DictType; key : KeyType ) : KeyType;
```
Any R-value subscript designator `dict[key]` is replaced by a function call `VALUE(dict, key)`.


### Primitive ATVALUE ###

Primitive `ATVALUE` implements R-value subscript designators for array types.

It returns the value stored at a given index in an indexed collection.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE ATVALUE ( CONST c : Collection; atIndex : LONGCARD ) : ValueType;
```
Any R-value subscript designator `a[i]` is replaced by a function call `ATVALUE(a, i)`.


### Primitive STORE ###

Primitive `STORE` implements L-value subscript designators for dictionary types.
It stores a given value for a given key in a key/value collection.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE STORE ( VAR dict : DictType; key : KeyType; value : ValueType );
```
A statement of the form `dict[key] := value` is replaced by a procedure call `STORE(dict, key, value)`.


### Primitive ATSTORE ###

Primitive `ATSTORE` implements L-value subscript designators for array types.
It stores a given value at a given index in an indexed collection.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE ATSTORE ( VAR c : Collection; atIndex : LONGCARD; value : ValueType );
```
A statement of the form `a[i] := v` is replaced by a procedure call `ATSTORE(a, i, v)`.


### Primitive ATINSERT ###

Primitive `ATINSERT` implements L-value subscript insertion designators.
It inserts one or more values into an indexed collection starting at a given index.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE ATINSERT ( VAR c : Collection; atIndex : LONGCARD; values : ARGLIST OF ValueType );
```
A statement of the form `a[i..] := v` is replaced by a procedure call `ATINSERT(a, i, v)`.


### Primitive ATREMOVE ###

Primitive `ATREMOVE` implements syntax for indexed value removal.
It removes a range of values from an indexed collection within a given index range.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE ATREMOVE ( VAR c : Collection; startIndex, endIndex : LONGCARD );
```
For values `a` of an array type, `s` of a string type, and `c` of any indexed collection type
* a statement of the form `a := {}` is replaced by a procedure call `ATREMOVE(a, 0, COUNT(a)-1)`
* a statement of the form `s := ""` is replaced by a procedure call `ATREMOVE(s, 0, LENGTH(s)-1)`
* a statement of the form `REMOVE(c, i, j)` is replaced by a procedure call `ATREMOVE(c, i, j)`


### Primitive ALLOC ###

Primitive `ALLOC` implements type independent memory allocation for `NEW` statements.

It allocates a memory block of a given size in octets and passes a pointer to it in its first argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE ALLOC ( VAR p : CAST ADDRESS; size : LONGCARD );
```

### Primitive DEALLOC ###

Primitive `DEALLOC` implements type independent memory deallocation for `RELEASE` statements.

It deallocates a memory block pointed to by its argument and passes `NIL` back therein.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE DEALLOC ( VAR p : CAST ADDRESS );
```


### Primitive STDIN ###

Primitive `STDIN` inserts the default value for an omitted input channel in `READ` statements.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE STDIN : LibraryDefinedChannelType;
```


### Primitive STDOUT ###

Primitive `STDOUT` inserts the default value for an omitted output channel in `WRITE` statements.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE STDOUT : LibraryDefinedChannelType;
```


## Restrictions ##

Predefined constants may be passed to function `UNSAFE.CAST()` but may not be passed to functions `PTR()` and `UNSAFE.ADR()`. Predefined procedures, functions and macros may not be assigned and may not be passed as arguments to any procedure, function or macro.

---

# Chapter 10 : Syntax Binding #
> This is the chapter headline

Syntax Binding is the process of mapping library defined procedures to built-in syntax. A library defined procedure is bound to a given built-in syntax form by including a binding specifier within the procedure’s header. The binding specifier determines to which syntax form the procedure is bound, and it must be included in both the procedure’s definition and declaration.

```antlr
procedureHeader :=
  PROCEDURE ( '[' bindingSpecifier ']')? procedureSignature ;

bindingSpecifier :=
  NEW ( ARGLIST | CAPACITY )? | RETAIN | RELEASE |
  READ NEW? | WRITE ( '#' )? | bindableIdent ;

bindableIdent := Pervasive | Primitive ;
```
A syntax form to which library defined procedures may be bound is said to be bindable. Procedures bound to it are called its bindings. While bindable syntax forms are type agnostic, their bindings are type specific, that is to say, each binding supports arguments of a specific type. For each type to be supported by a bindable syntax form, a binding specific to arguments of the type is required.

A bindable syntax form `S` is said to have a binding for type `T` when a library procedure `p` that supports arguments of `T` has been bound to `S`. Procedure `p` is said to be bound to `S` in respect of `T`.


## Syntax Transformation ##

Bindable syntax forms act as [[Wirthian macro|Language-Description#wirthian-macro]]s. A syntax form `S` with binding `p` for type `T`, an occurrence of `S` with a primary argument or argument list `a` of type `T` is resolved into a procedure call to `p`, passing `a`.

`S a ...` &longrightarrow; `T.p(a...)`



## Memory Management Bindings ##

### Binding to NEW ###

Statement `NEW` has three bindable syntax forms.

### NEW without Arguments ###

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [NEW] New ( VAR p : Foo );
```
binds ADT library procedure `Foo.New()` to the `NEW` statement in respect of type `Foo`.

Any use of the `NEW` statement with a designator of type `Foo` and without initialiser nor capacity specifier is then
replaced by a call to procedure `Foo.New()`.

Given the following import and declaration within a client module

```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo; VAR foo : Foo;
```
a statement of the form

```Modula-2
NEW foo;
```
is resolved into a library call

```Modula-2
Foo.New(foo);
```


### NEW with Initialiser ###

An ADT module `Foo` with the binding

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [NEW ARGLIST] NewWithArgs ( VAR p : Foo; initVal : ARGLIST OF Value );
```
bind ADT library procedure `Foo.NewWithArgs()` to the `NEW` statement in respect of type `Foo`.

Any use of the `NEW` statement with a designator of type `Foo` and an initialiser is then resolved into a call to
procedure `Foo.NewWithArgs()`.

Given the following import and declaration within a client module
```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo; VAR foo : Foo;
```
a statement of the form
```Modula-2
NEW foo := { a, b, c };
```
is resolved into a library call
```Modula-2
Foo.NewWithArgs(foo, a, b, c);
```


### NEW with Capacity ###

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [NEW CAPACITY] NewWithCapacity ( VAR p : Foo; capacity : LONGCARD );
```
binds ADT library procedure `Foo.NewWithCapacity()` to the `NEW` statements in respect of type `Foo`.

Any use of the `NEW` statement with a designator of type `Foo` and a capacity specifier is then resolved into a call to procedure `Foo.NewWithCapacity()`.

Given the following import and declaration within a client module

```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo; VAR foo : Foo;
```

a statement of the form
```Modula-2
NEW foo CAPACITY n;
```
is resolved into a library call
```Modula-2
Foo.NewWithCapacity(foo, n);
```


### Binding to RETAIN ###

An ADT module Foo with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [RETAIN] Retain ( p : Foo );
```
binds ADT library procedure `Retain()` to the `RETAIN` statement in respect of type `Foo`.

Any use of `RETAIN` with a designator of type `Foo` is then resolved into a call to procedure `Foo.Retain()`.


Given the following import and declaration within a client module
```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo; VAR foo : Foo;
```
a statement of the form
```Modula-2
RETAIN foo;
```
is resolved into a library cal
```Modula-2
Foo.Retain(foo);
```


### Binding to RELEASE ##

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [RELEASE] Release ( VAR p : Foo );
```
binds ADT library procedure `Release()` to the `RELEASE` statement in respect of type `Foo`.

Given the following import and declaration within a client module
```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo; VAR foo : Foo;
```
a statement of the form
```Modula-2
RELEASE foo;
```
is resolved into a library cal
```Modula-2
Foo.Release(foo);
```


## I/O Bindings ##

### Binding to READ ###

Statement `READ` has two associated bindings.
* `[READ]` bindings are used to generate calls for **plain** designators in a `READ` statement
* `[READ NEW]` bindings are used to generate calls for `NEW`-prefixed designators in a `READ` statement

An IO module `FooIO` with a binding

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [READ] Read ( chan : IOChannel; VAR value : Foo );
```
binds IO library procedure `Read()` to the `READ` statement in respect of type `Foo`.

Any plain designator of type `Foo` within a `READ` statement will then generate a call to procedure `FooIO.Read()`.

An IO module `FooIO` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [READ NEW] ReadNew ( chan : IOChannel; VAR ptr : Foo ); (* Foo is a pointer type *)
```
binds IO library procedure `ReadNew()` to the `READ` statement in respect of pointer type `Foo`.

Any `NEW`-prefixed designator of pointer type `Foo` within a `READ` statement will then generate a call to procedure `FooIO.ReadNew()`.


#### READ with Explicit IO Channel ####

Given the following imports and declaration within a client module
```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo, FooIO, IOChannel; VAR foo : Foo; file : IOChannel;
```
* a statement of the form `READ @file : foo` is resolved into a library call `FooIO.Read(file, foo)`
* a statement of the form `READ @file : NEW foo` is resolved into a library call `FooIO.ReadNew(file, foo)`


#### READ with Default IO Channel ####

If the IO channel argument is omitted, default input channel primitive `STDIN` is automatically inserted. In order to resolve `STDIN` an appropriate IO library must be imported to bind an input channel to `STDIN` and make it visible within the current module scope. Import of module `StdIO` binds `StdIO.in()` to `STDIN`.

Given the following imports and declaration within a client module
```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo, FooIO, StdIO; VAR foo : Foo;
```
* a statement of the form `READ foo` is resolved into a library call `FooIO.Read(StdIO.in(), foo)`
* a statement of the form `READ NEW foo` is resolved into a library call `FooIO.ReadNew(StdIO.in(), foo)`


#### READ with Multiple Arguments ####

Given modules `Foo`, `Bar`, `Baz` and `Bam`, their respective IO modules `FooIO`, `BarIO`, `BazIO` and `BamIO`
with bindings to for their respective types and the following imports and declaration within a client module
```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo, FooIO, Bar, BarIO, Baz, BazIO, Bam, BamIO, StdIO;
VAR foo : Foo; bar : Bar; baz : Baz; bam : Bam;
```
a statement of the form
```Modula-2
READ foo, NEW bar, baz, NEW bam;
```
is resolved into a statement sequence
```Modula-2
FooIO.Read(StdIO.in(), foo); BarIO.ReadNew(StdIO.in(), bar);
BazIO.Read(StdIO.in(), baz); BamIO.ReadNew(StdIO.in(), bam);
```


#### Binding to WRITE ####

Statement `WRITE` has two associated bindings.
* `[WRITE]` bindings are used to generate calls for **unformatted** output values in a `WRITE` statement
* `[WRITE #]` bindings are used to generate calls for **formatted** output values in a `WRITE` statement

An IO module `FooIO` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [WRITE] Write ( chan : IOChannel; value : Foo );
```
binds IO library procedure `Write()` to the `WRITE` statement in respect of type `Foo`.

Any unformatted output value of type `Foo` within a `WRITE` statement will then generate a call to procedure
`FooIO.Write()`.

An IO module `FooIO` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [WRITE #] WriteF ( chan : IOChannel; CONST fmtStr : ARRAY OF CHAR; value : Foo );
```
binds IO library procedure `WriteF()` to the `WRITE` statement in respect of type `Foo`.

Any formatted output value of type `Foo` within a `WRITE` statement will then generate a call to procedure
`FooIO.WriteF()`.


#### WRITE with Explicit IO Channel ####

Given the following imports and declaration within a client module
```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo, FooIO, IOChannel; VAR foo : Foo; file : IOChannel;
```
* a statement of the form `WRITE @file : foo` is resolved into a library cal `FooIO.Write(file, foo)`
* a statement of the form `WRITE @file : #(fmt, foo)` is resolved into a library call `FooIO.WriteF(file, fmt, foo)`


#### WRITE with Default IO Channel ####

If the IO channel argument is omitted, default output channel primitive `STDOUT` is automatically inserted. In order to resolve `STDOUT` an appropriate IO library must be imported to bind an output channel to `STDOUT` and make it visible within the current module scope. Import of module `StdIO` binds `StdIO.out()` to `STDOUT`.

Given the following imports and declaration within a client module
* a statement of the form `WRITE foo` is resolved into a library call `FooIO.Write(StdIO.out(), foo)`
* a statement of the form `WRITE #(fmt, foo)` is resolved into a call `FooIO.WriteF(StdIO.out(), fmt, foo)`


#### WRITE with Multiple Arguments ####

Given modules `Foo`, `Bar`, `Baz` and `Bam`, their respective IO modules `FooIO`, `BarIO`, `BazIO` and `BamIO`
with bindings to for their respective types and the following imports and declaration within a client module

```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo, FooIO, Bar, BarIO, Baz, BazIO, Bam, BamIO, StdIO;
VAR foo : Foo; bar : Bar; baz : Baz; bam : Bam;
```
a statement of the form
```Modula-2
WRITE foo, #(fmt1, bar), baz, #(fmt2, bam);
```
is resolved into a statement sequence
```Modula-2
FooIO.Write(StdIO.out(), foo); BarIO.WriteF(StdIO.out(), fmt1, bar);
BazIO.Write(StdIO.out(), baz); BarIO.WriteF(StdIO.out(), fmt2, bam);
```


## Binding to Pervasives ##

### Binding to Procedure APPEND ##


An ADT module `Foo` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [APPEND] Append ( VAR a : Foo; values : ARGLIST OF Value );
```
binds ADT library procedure `Foo.Append()` to pervasive procedure `APPEND()` in respect of type `Foo`.

Any call to `APPEND()` with a first argument of type `Foo` is then resolved into a call to procedure `Foo.Append()`.

Given the following import and declaration within a client module
```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo; VAR foo : Foo;
```
an invocation of the form `APPEND(foo, v1, v2, v3)` is resolved into a library call `Foo.Append(foo, v1, v2, v3)`.


### Binding to Procedure REMOVE ##

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [REMOVE] Remove ( VAR a : Foo; keys : ARGLIST OF Keys );
```
binds ADT library procedure `Foo.Remove()` to pervasive procedure `REMOVE()` in respect of type `Foo`.

Any call to `REMOVE()` with a first argument of type `Foo` is then resolved into a call to procedure `Foo.Remove()`.

Given the following import and declaration within a client module
```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo; VAR foo : Foo;
```
an invocation of the form `REMOVE(foo, k1, k2, k3)` is resolved into a library call `Foo.Append(foo, k1, k2, k3)`.


### Binding to Function COUNT ##

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [COUNT] count ( VAR a : Foo ) : LONGCARD;
```
binds ADT library function `Foo.count()` to pervasive function `COUNT()` in respect of type `Foo`.

Any invocation of `COUNT()` with an argument of type `Foo` is then resolved into a call to function `Foo.count()`.

Given the following import and declaration within a client module
```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo; VAR foo : Foo;
```
an invocation of the form `COUNT(foo)` is resolved into a library call `Foo.count(foo)`.

Within any given ADT module, bindings to `COUNT` and `LENGTH` are mutually exclusive.


### Binding to Function LENGTH ##

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [LENGTH] length ( VAR a : Foo ) : LONGCARD;
```
binds ADT library function `Foo.length()` to pervasive function `LENGTH()` in respect of type `Foo`.

Any invocation of `LENGTH()` with an argument of type `Foo` is then resolved into a call to function `Foo.length()`.

Given the following import and declaration within a client module
```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo; VAR foo : Foo;
```
an invocation of the form `LENGTH(foo)` is resolved into a library call `Foo.length(foo)`.

Within any given ADT module, bindings to `COUNT` and `LENGTH` are mutually exclusive.


### Binding to Function FIRST ##

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [FIRST] firstKey ( a : Foo ) : KeyType (* of Foo *);
```
binds ADT library function `Foo.firstKey()` to pervasive function `FIRST()` in respect of type `Foo`.

Any invocation of `FIRST()` with an argument of type `Foo` is then resolved into a call to function `Foo.firstKey()`.

Given the following import and declaration within a client module
```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo; VAR foo : Foo;
```
an invocation of the form `FIRST(foo)` is resolved into a library call `Foo.firstKey(foo)`.


### Binding to Function LAST ##

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [LAST] lastKey ( a : Foo ) : KeyType (* of Foo *);
```
binds ADT library function `Foo.lastKey()` to pervasive function `LAST()` in respect of type `Foo`.

Any invocation of `LAST()` with an argument of type `Foo` is then resolved into a call to function `Foo.lastKey()`.

Given the following import and declaration within a client module
```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo; VAR foo : Foo;
```
an invocation of the form `LAST(foo)` is resolved into a library call `Foo.lastKey(foo)`.


### Binding to Function PREV ##

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [PREV] prevKey ( VAR a : Foo; key : Key ) : Key;
```
binds ADT library function `Foo.prevKey()` to pervasive function `PREV()` in respect of type `Foo`.

Any invocation of `PREV()` with an argument of type `Foo` is then resolved into a call to function `Foo.prevKey()`.

Given the following import and declaration within a client module
```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo; VAR foo : Foo; key : Foo.Key;
```
an invocation of the form `PREV(foo, key)` is resolved into a library call `Foo.prevKey(foo, key)`.


### Binding to Function NEXT ##

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [NEXT] nextKey ( VAR a : Foo; key : Key ) : Key;
```
binds ADT library function `Foo.nextKey()` to pervasive function `NEXT()` in respect of type `Foo`.

Any invocation of `NEXT()` with an argument of type `Foo` is then resolved into a call to function `Foo.nextKey()`.

Given the following import and declaration within a client module
```Modula-2
IMPORT Foo; VAR foo : Foo; key : Foo.Key;
```
an invocation of the form `NEXT(foo, key)` is resolved into a library call `Foo.nextKey(foo, key)`.


### Binding to Macro TLIMIT ##

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding
```Modula-2
CONST [TLIMIT] Capacity = 1000;
```
binds library constant `Foo.Capacity` to pervasive macro `TLIMIT()` in respect of type `Foo`.

Given an import directive `IMPORT Foo;` within a client module,

an expression of the form `TLIMIT(Foo)` is replaced by library constant `Foo.Capacity`.
 

## Binding to the Collation Table ##

A library module may bind an array constant to the language processor’s internal collation table.
```Modula-2
CONST [COLLATION] Order = { index0, index1, index2, ... index94 };
```
where value `Order[i]` defines the collation index of character `CHR(i+32)` for `i` in range `[0..94]`.

Such a library is called a collation library. Import of a collation library causes pervasive function `COLLATION()` and thereby all string comparisons to use the library defined order within the scope of the importing module.


## Binding to Primitives ##

### Binding to Primitive VALUE ###

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [VALUE] valueForKey ( VAR a : Foo; key : Key ) : Value;
```
binds function `Foo.valueForKey()` to primitive `VALUE` in respect of type `Foo`.

Any (internal) use of primitive `VALUE` with a first argument of type `Foo` is then resolved into a library call to
function `Foo.valueForKey()` during translation of syntax forms that are synthesised using primitive `VALUE`.


### Binding to Primitive ATVALUE ###

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [ATVALUE] valueAtIndex ( VAR a : Foo; atIndex : LONGCARD ) : Value;
```
binds function `Foo.valueAtIndex()` to primitive `ATVALUE` in respect of type `Foo`.

Any (internal) use of primitive `ATVALUE` with a first argument of type `Foo` is then resolved into a library call to
function `Foo.valueAtIndex()` during translation of syntax forms that are synthesised using primitive `ATVALUE`.


### Binding to Primitive STORE ###

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [STORE] StoreKeyValue ( VAR a : Foo; key : Key; value : Value );
```
binds procedure `Foo.StoreKeyValue` to primitive `STORE` in respect of type `Foo`.

Any (internal) use of primitive `STORE` with a first argument of type `Foo` is then resolved into a library call to
procedure `Foo.StoreKeyValue` during translation of syntax forms that are synthesised using primitive `STORE`.


### Binding to Primitive ATSTORE ###

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [ATSTORE] StoreAtIndex ( VAR a : Foo; atIndex : LONGCARD; value : Value );
```
binds procedure `Foo.StoreAtIndex` to primitive `ATSTORE` in respect of type `Foo`.

Any (internal) use of primitive `ATSTORE` with a first argument of type `Foo` is then resolved into a library call to
procedure `Foo.StoreAtIndex` during translation of syntax forms that are synthesised using primitive `ATSTORE`.


### Binding to Primitive ATINSERT ###

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [ATINSERT] AtInsert ( VAR a : Foo; atIndex : LONGCARD; values : ARGLIST OF Value );
```
binds procedure `Foo.AtInsert` to primitive `ATINSERT` in respect of type `Foo`.

Any (internal) use of primitive `ATINSERT` with a first argument of type `Foo` is then resolved into a library call to
procedure `Foo.AtInsert` during translation of syntax forms that are synthesised using primitive `ATINSERT`.


### Binding to Primitive ATREMOVE ###

An ADT module `Foo` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [ATREMOVE] AtRemove ( VAR a : Foo; startIndex, endIndex : LONGCARD );
```
binds procedure `Foo.AtRemove` to primitive `ATREMOVE` in respect of type `Foo`.

Any (internal) use of primitive `ATREMOVE` with a first argument of type `Foo` is then resolved into a library call to
procedure `Foo.AtRemove` during translation of syntax forms that are synthesised using primitive `ATREMOVE`.


### Binding to Primitive ALLOC ###

A storage library module `MyStorage` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [ALLOC] Allocate ( VAR p : CAST ADDRESS; size : LONGCARD );
```
binds procedure `MyStorage.Allocate` to primitive `ALLOC`.

Any (internal) use of primitive `ALLOC` is then resolved into a library call to procedure `MyStorage.Allocate` during translation of `NEW` statements for types that do not provide type specific bindings to `NEW`.


### Binding to Primitive DEALLOC ###

A storage library module `MyStorage` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [DEALLOC] Deallocate ( VAR p : CAST ADDRESS );
```
binds procedure `MyStorage.Deallocate` to primitive `DEALLOC`.

Any (internal) use of primitive `DEALLOC` is then resolved into a library call to procedure `MyStorage.Deallocate` during translation of `RELEASE` statements for types that do not provide type specific bindings to `RELEASE`.


### Binding to Primitive STDIN ###

An IO module `MyIO` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [STDIN] stdIn : ArbitraryIOChannelType;
```
binds `MyIO.stdIn()` to primitive `STDIN`. (Internal) use of `STDIN` is then resolved to `MyIO.stdIn()`.


### Binding to Primitive STDOUT ###

An IO module `MyIO` with a binding
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE [STDOUT] stdOut : ArbitraryIOChannelType;
```
binds `MyIO.stdOut()` to primitive `STDOUT`. (Internal) use of `STDOUT` is then resolved to `MyIO.stdOut()`.

---

# Chapter 11 : Low-Level Facilities #
> This is the chapter headline

Low-level facilities are provided by special module `UNSAFE`. Although presented as a library it is built into the language. Its facilities are potentially unsafe, their use may bypass the type safety of the language and they must therefore be used with caution. The module must be imported before its facilities can be used.

Module `UNSAFE` provides the following identifiers:

[`BitsPerByte`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#bitsperbyte),
[`BytesPerWord`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#bytesperword),
[`BytesPerLongWord`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#bytesperlongword),
[`BitsPerAddress`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#bitsperaddress),
[`MachineTypeMaxBits`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#machinebytesmaxbits),
[`BitOpsMaxBits`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#bitopsmaxbits),
[`BYTE`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#byte),
[`WORD`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#word),
[`LONGWORD`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#longword),
[`ADDRESS`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#address),
[`ADD`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#add),
[`SUB`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#sub),
[`SETBIT`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#setbit),
[`HALT`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#halt),
[`ADR`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#adr),
[`CAST`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#cast),
[`BIT`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#bit),
[`SHL`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#shl),
[`SHR`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#shr),
[`ASHR`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#ashr),
[`BWNOT`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#bwnot),
[`BWAND`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#bwand),
[`BWOR`](https://github.com/m2sf/m2bsk/wiki/Language-Description#bwor);


## Low-Level Constants ##

### BitsPerByte ###

Constant `BitsPerByte` represents the bit width of the smallest addressable machine unit of the target architecture measured in bits. Its value is therefore target architecture dependent. For the overwhelming majority of platforms in use this value will be eight.

Memory allocation in Modula-2 is strictly in units of eight bits and multiples thereof. On (legacy) targets where the smallest addressable machine unit is not eight bits, the mapping of type `OCTET` to machine types is implementation defined.

### BytesPerWord ###

Constant `BytesPerWord` represents the size of a machine register of the target architecture smaller than the
largest machine register. Its value is target architecture dependent.

### BytesPerLongWord ###

Constant `BytesPerLongWord` represents the size of the largest machine register of the target architecture. Its value is target architecture dependent. If the target architecture does not support machine registers of different sizes, `BytesPerLongWord` is equal to `BytesPerWord`.

### BitsPerAddress ###

Constant `BitsPerAddress` represents the bit width of type `ADDRESS`.

### MachineTypeMaxBits ###

Constant `MachineTypeMaxBits` represents the bit width of the largest machine type.

### BitOpsMaxBits ###

Constant `BitOpsMaxBits` represents the bit width of the largest operand supported by the `UNSAFE` bit operations `ADD()`, `SUB()`, `SETBIT()`, `BIT()`, `SHL()`, `SHR()`, `ASHR()`, `BWNOT()`, `BWAND()` and `BWOR()`.


## Low-Level Types ##

### BYTE ###

Type `BYTE` is a machine type.

It represents the smallest addressable storage unit of the target architecture.

Its bit width is defined by constant `BitsPerByte`.

Its value range is `[0..POW2(BitsPerByte)-1]`.

Whole number literals are compatible with type `BYTE`.


### WORD ###

Type `WORD` is a machine type.

It represents a storage unit equivalent to the size of a machine register of the target architecture.

Its bit width is defined by constants `BitsPerByte` and `BytesPerWord`.

Its value range is `[0..POW2(BitsPerByte*BytesPerWord)-1]`.

Whole number literals are compatible with type `WORD`.


### LONGWORD ###

Type `LONGWORD` is a machine type.

It represents a storage unit equivalent to the size of the largest machine register of the target architecture.

Its bit width is defined by constants `BitsPerByte` and `BytesPerLongWord`.

Its value range is `[0..POW2(BitsPerByte*BytesPerLongWord)-1]`.

Whole number literals are compatible with type `LONGWORD`.


### ADDRESS ###

Type `ADDRESS` is a (semi-abstract) reference type.

It represents the addressable memory space available to the Modula-2 runtime environment.

Its bit width is defined by constant `BitsPerAddress`.

Its value range is `[0..POW2(BitsPerAddress)-1]`.

Whole number literals and `NIL` are compatible with type `ADDRESS`.

#### Address Mapping ###

On platforms where the smallest addressable storage unit is eight bits, type `ADDRESS` represents machine addresses of the target architecture. On (legacy) platforms where the smallest addressable unit is not eight bits, the type represents abstract addresses that need to be mapped to the platform’s actual address space. On such platforms, address mapping is implementation defined.

Regardless of the underlying architecture, type `ADDRESS` is classified as a machine type.


#### Dual Representation ####

Type `ADDRESS` exhibits type duality. It is presented both as a pointer type and as an octet sequence. It may thus be thought of as having two overlapping definitions
```Modula-2
TYPE ADDRESS = (* Pseudo-Definition *)
| POINTER TO OCTET
| OCTETSEQ [POW2(BitsPerAddress)]
END; (* ADDRESS *)
```

##### Presentation as a Pointer #####

An entity `a` of type `ADDRESS` may be dereferenced using the `^` operator. An expression of the form `a^` is of type `OCTET` and represents the octet value stored at address `a`.
```Modula-2
adr := UNSAFE.ADR(x);
WRITE "octet value at address ", adr, " is ", adr^;
```

##### Presentation as a Stream #####

An entity `a` of type `ADDRESS` may also be referenced by subscript. An expression of the form `a[n]` is of type `OCTET` and represents the octet value stored at address `a+n`, where `n` is an offset of type `LONGCARD`. Negative indices are not supported.
```Modula-2
adr := UNSAFE.ADR(x);
WRITE "octet value at address ", adr, "[", offset, "] is ", adr[offset];
```

### Casting Formal Types ###

Import of module `UNSAFE` enables the use of casting formal type syntax `CAST` `ADDRESS` and `CAST` `OCTETSEQ` within the importing module scope. Without such import the syntax is unavailable and any attempt to use it will cause a syntax error at compile time.

#### CAST ADDRESS ####

An argument passed to a parameter of formal type `CAST` `ADDRESS` is cast to type `ADDRESS`. That is, within the body of the procedure, the argument is interpreted as a value of type `ADDRESS`. It must be of a pointer type.
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE Allocate ( VAR ptr : CAST ADDRESS; size : LONGCARD );
...
TYPE FooPtr = POINTER TO Foo; VAR foo : FooPtr;
Allocate(foo, TSIZE(Foo));
```
All pointer types are passing compatible to formal type `CAST` `ADDRESS`.

#### CAST OCTETSEQ ####

An argument passed to a parameter of formal type `CAST` `OCTETSEQ` is cast to an octet sequence*. That is, within the body of the procedure, the parameter is interpreted as a stream of values of type OCTET , individually addressable by subscript. The argument may be of any type.

[*] This replaces the implicit casting semantics of `ARRAY` `OF` `WORD` and `ARRAY` `OF` `LOC` in earlier versions of Modula-2.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE HexDump ( dataSeq : CAST OCTETSEQ );
...
VAR foo : Foo; bar : Bar; baz : Baz;
HexDump(foo), HexDump(bar); HexDump(baz);
```
Any entity regardless of its type is passing compatible to formal parameters of type `CAST` `OCTETSEQ`, but a parameter of type `OCTETSEQ` is passing compatible only to formal parameters of type `OCTETSEQ`.

When an argument is passed to a parameter of formal type `CAST` `OCTETSEQ`, the allocation size of the argument is passed as a hidden parameter of type `LONGCARD` immediately before the casting parameter.
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE HexDump ( (*hidden size : LONGCARD;*) dataSeq : CAST OCTETSEQ );
```
The value of the hidden parameter may be obtained within the procedure body by calling function `LENGTH` with the identifier of the casting parameter as its argument.
```Modula-2
LENGTH(dataSeq) (* number of octets passed to dataSeq *)
```

Operations on parameters of type `OCTETSEQ` are strictly limited to subscript, `FOR` iteration, `ADR()` , `CAST()` and `LENGTH()`. The limitation does not apply to the octet values of an octet sequence parameter.

##### Addressing by Subscript #####

The values of an octet sequence parameter are addressable by subscript. Negative indices are not supported.
```Modula-2
WRITE "octet value at index ", index, " is ", dataSeq[index];
```

##### Iteration by FOR Loop #####

An octet sequence parameter is iterable by `FOR` loop.
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE HexDump ( VAR dataSeq : CAST OCTETSEQ );
BEGIN
  FOR value IN dataSeq DO
    WRITE value, " "
  END (* FOR *)
  WRITE "\n", LENGTH(dataSeq), " values\n"
END HexDump;
```


## Low-Level Procedures ##

### ADD ###

Procedure `ADD` interprets the bit patterns of its operands as unsigned numbers and adds the value of the second to the first operand, ignoring overflow.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE ADD ( VAR x : T1; y : T2 );
```
where `T1`, `T2` &in; *T*<sub>*builtin*</sub> &#8726; *T*<sub>*real*</sub>
&and; **tsize** `T1` &ge; **tsize** `T2`


### SUB ###

Procedure `SUB` interprets the bit patterns of its operands as unsigned numbers and subtracts the value of the second from the first operand, ignoring underflow.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE SUB ( VAR x : T1; y : T2 );
```
where `T1`, `T2` &in; *T*<sub>*builtin*</sub> &#8726; *T*<sub>*real*</sub>
&and; **tsize** `T1` &ge; **tsize** `T2`


### SETBIT ###

Procedure `SETBIT` sets the bit at the zero-based index given by its second argument of the value given by its first argument to the bit value given by its third argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE SETBIT ( VAR target : T1; bitIndex : T2; bit : BOOLEAN );
```
where `T1`, `T2` &in; *T*<sub>*builtin*</sub>
&and; `T2` &in; *T*<sub>*cardinal*</sub>
&and; 0 &le; `bitIndex` &le; 8 &middot; **tsize** `T1`

The bit index of the least significant bit is always zero.


### HALT ###

Procedure `HALT` aborts the running program and passes an exit value to its host environment. On host environments that do not support an exit value, the exit value is discarded. On host environments that support an exit value, the range and meaning of exit values is dependent on the host environment.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE HALT ( exitValue : T );
```
where `T` &in; *T*<sub>*whole*</sub>


## Low-Level Functions ##

### ADR ###

Function `ADR` returns the address of its argument.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE ADR ( value : T ) : ADDRESS;
```
where `T` &in; *T*<sub>*any*</sub>


### CAST ###

Function `CAST` represents an unsafe type transfer operation. It returns the value of its second argument, interpreted as if it was of the type given by its first argument, regardless of whether the value makes sense for the target type or not.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE CAST ( TypeIdent; value : T1 ) : T2;
```
where `T` &in; *T*<sub>*any*</sub>
&and; `TypeIdent` = **id** `T2`
&and; **tsize** `T1` &le; **tsize** `T2`

If the second argument is a compile time expression, then it must be any of `NIL`, `TRUE` or `FALSE`, or it must be an explicitly typed expression of the form `expr :: T`.

If the allocation size of the second argument is smaller than the allocation size of the target type, the higher bits will be filled with zeroes up to the bit width of the target type.


### BIT ###

Function `BIT` returns `TRUE` if the bit at the zero-based index given by its second argument of the value given by its first argument is set, otherwise `FALSE`.

```Modula-2
PROCEDURE BIT ( value : T1; bitIndex : T2 ) : BOOLEAN;
```
where `T1` &in; *T*<sub>*builtin*</sub>
&and; `T2` &in; *T*<sub>*cardinal*</sub>
&and; 0 &le; `bitIndex` &lt; 8 &middot; **tsize** `T1`

The bit index of the least significant bit is always zero.


### SHL ###

Function `SHL` returns the value of its first argument bit-shifted to the left by the number of positions passed in its second argument.
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE SHL ( value : T1; shiftFactor : T2 ) : T3;
```
where `T1` &in; *T*<sub>*builtin*</sub>
&and; `T2` &in; *T*<sub>*cardinal*</sub>
&and; `T3` = `T1`

Bits that are shifted out to the left are discarded and will not cause overflow.


### SHR ###

Function `SHR` returns the value of its first argument bit-shifted **logically** to the right by the number of positions passed in its second argument.
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE SHR ( value : T1; shiftFactor : T2 ) : T3;
```
where `T1` &in; *T*<sub>*builtin*</sub>
&and; `T2` &in; *T*<sub>*cardinal*</sub>
&and; `T3` = `T1`

Bits that are shifted out to the right are discarded and will not cause underflow.


### ASHR ###

Function `ASHR` returns the value of its first argument bit-shifted **arithmetically** to the right by the number of positions passed in its second argument.
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE ASHR ( value : T1; shiftFactor : T2 ) : T3;
```
where `T1` &in; *T*<sub>*builtin*</sub>
&and; `T2` &in; *T*<sub>*cardinal*</sub>
&and; `T3` = `T1`

Bits that are shifted out to the right are discarded and will not cause underflow.


### BWNOT ###

Function `BWNOT` returns the bitwise logical negation of its argument.
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE BWNOT ( value : T1 ) : T2;
```
where `T1` &in; *T*<sub>*builtin*</sub>
&and; `T2` = `T1`


### BWAND ###

Function `BWAND` returns the bitwise logical conjunction of its first argument and a bit mask given by its second argument.
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE BWAND ( value : T1; mask : T2 ) : T3;
```
where `T1` &in; *T*<sub>*builtin*</sub>
&and; `T2` &in; *T*<sub>*cardinal*</sub>
&and; **tsize** `T2` = **tsize** `T1`
&and; `T3` = `T1`


### BWOR ###

Function `BWOR` returns the bitwise logical disjunction of its first argument and a bit mask given by its second argument.
```Modula-2
PROCEDURE BWOR ( value : T1; mask : T2 ) : T3;
```
where `T1` &in; *T*<sub>*builtin*</sub>
&and; `T2` &in; *T*<sub>*cardinal*</sub>
&and; **tsize** `T2` = **tsize** `T1`
&and; `T3` = `T1`

## Restrictions ##

Procedures and functions provided by module `UNSAFE` may not be assigned and may not be passed as arguments to any procedure, function or macro.

---

# Chapter 12 : File Name Extensions #

In order to allow portability of source code libraries between different language processors, their file name extensions shall be standardised as follows:

* `.if` for source files representing interface modules
* `.mod` for source files representing implementation and program modules
* `.prag` for pragma files containing pragmas to control compiler settings **(optional)**

---

# Terms of Reference #

### Allocation Size ###

The *allocation size* of a type *T*, denoted as **tsize** *T* is the size of the memory required to allocate an instance of&nbsp;*T*. It is measured in units of 8 bits called octets.


### Capacity ###

The *capacity* of a collection *c* is the maximal number of values that can be stored in *c*. Its value is determined by the type of of *c*. Its definition is
* **capacity** *c* = **tlimit** *T* where *T* is the type of *c*.

The *capacity* of a ***pass-by-value*** open array parameter *p<sub>val</sub>* is equivalent to the [[cardinality|Language-Description#cardinality]] or [[value count|Language-Description#value-count]] of the collection passed to *p<sub>val</sub>*. Its definition is
* **capacity** *p<sub>val</sub>* = **count** *c* where *c* is the collection passed to *p<sub>val</sub>*.

The *capacity* of a ***pass-by-reference*** open array parameter *p<sub>ref</sub>* is equivalent to the capacity of the collection passed to *p<sub>ref</sub>*. Its definition is
* **capacity** *p<sub>ref</sub>* = **capacity** *c* where *c* is the collection passed to *p<sub>ref</sub>*.


### Capacity Limit ###

The *capacity limit* of a collection type *T*, denoted as **tlimit** *T* is the maximum number of items that an instance of *T* can store. A capacity limit of zero indicates that the collection type does not impose any hard limit.


### Cardinality ###

In mathematics, the *cardinality* of a set *A*, denoted as |*A*| or **card** *A* is a measure of the number of elements in *A*. In this specification, the definition is extended to include collections, open array parameters and variadic argument lists.
* the cardinality of a collection *c* is the number of components stored in *c*
* the cardinality of an open array parameter *p* is that of of the collection passed in *p*
* the cardinality of a variadic argument list *v* is the number of arguments passed in *v*


### Compatibility ###

*Compatibility* is a property that determines whether one entity may be assigned to, copied to, passed to or used in an expression with another entity.

* a literal is *fully compatible* with type *T* if it is assignment, copy, by&#8209;value and by&#8209;reference passing compatible to and expression compatible with type&nbsp;*T*
* type *T*<sub>*1*</sub> is *fully compatible* with type *T*<sub>*2*</sub> if it is assignment, copy, by&#8209;value and by&#8209;reference passing compatible to and expression compatible with type&nbsp;*T*<sub>*2*</sub>


### Compatibility, Assignment ###

*Assignment compatibility* is a property that determines whether one entity may be assigned to another entity.
